text: Let \(n\) positive integer representing number environmental variables influence population growth model, .e., \(n\) dimension \(\vec{e}_t\). Let \(\vec{\mu}=(\mu_1,\ldots,\mu_n)\) \(n\)-vector unconstrained real values representing optimal values population growth environmental variable. Let \(\vec{\sigma}_L=(\sigma_{L,1},\ldots,\sigma_{L,n})\) \(\vec{\sigma}_R=(\sigma_{R,1},\ldots,\sigma_{R,n})\) \(n\)-vectors strictly positive real values representing widths growth-environment function left right, respectively, respect axes. Let \(O\) \(n \times n\) orthogonal matrix (.e., \(OO^\tau = \), \(\tau\) represents matrix transpose) encoding axes used. Let \(\lambda_{\text{max}}\) represent population growth optimal conditions, .e., maximum possible annual net growth rate. growth-environment function defined \[\begin{equation} \lambda_t = \lambda_{\text{max}} \prod_{=1}^n \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{u_{t,}}{\sigma_i(u_{t,})}  \right)^2 \right), \label{eq:gefunc} \end{equation}\] \(\vec{u}_t = O^{-1}(\vec{e}_t-\vec{\mu})\) \(\sigma_i(u_{t,}) = \sigma_{L,}\) \(u_{t,} \leq 0\) \(\sigma_i(u_{t,}) = \sigma_{R,}\) \(u_{t,} > 0\). figure shows examples functions can obtained \(\eqref{eq:gefunc}\) one two dimensions. now elaborate reasoning behind \(\eqref{eq:gefunc}\) parameters control shape function. univariate case (\(n=1\)), \(\eqref{eq:gefunc}\) reduces \[ \lambda_t= \lambda_{\text{max}}\exp \left( -\frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{e_t - \mu}{\sigma(e_t-\mu)} \right) ^2 \right), \] \(\sigma(e_t-\mu) = \sigma_L\) \(e_t \leq \mu\), \(\sigma(e_t-\mu) = \sigma_R\) \(e_t > \mu\). function proportional asymmetric generalization probability density function (pdf) normal distribution. form convenient annual net growth rate decline monotonically zero extreme values \(e_t\), though may asymmetrically. similar reasons, multivariate growth-environment function asymmetric generalization functional form used pdf multivariate normal distribution, \[\begin{equation} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2}(\vec{e}_t-\vec{\mu})^\tau \Sigma^{-1} (\vec{e}_t-\vec{\mu}) \right), \label{eq:multivarnorm} \end{equation}\] \(\Sigma\) positive-definite covariance matrix. define asymmetric generalization eq. \(\eqref{eq:multivarnorm}\), first note, spectral theorem, can write \(\Sigma= O D O^{-1}\) \(O\) orthogonal matrix \(D\) diagonal matrix positive diagonal entries. columns \(O\) eigenvectors \(\Sigma\) diagonal entries \(D\) corresponding eigenvalues. , \[ \begin{aligned} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\vec{e}_t - \vec{\mu})^\tau \Sigma^{-1} (\vec{e}_t - \vec{\mu})\right) &= \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\vec{e}_t - \vec{\mu})^\tau O D^{-1} O^{-1}  (\vec{e}_t - \vec{\mu})\right) \\ &= \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}[O^{-1} (\vec{e}_t - \vec{\mu})]^\tau D^{-1} [O^{-1} (\vec{e}_t - \vec{\mu})]\right) \\ &= \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}\vec{u}_t^\tau D^{-1} \vec{u}_t\right), \end{aligned} \] \(\vec{u}_t = O^{-1} (\vec{e}_t - \vec{\mu})\). Letting \(\sigma_i\) square root \(\)th diagonal entry \(D\), \[ \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{=1}^n \left( \frac{u_{t,}}{\sigma_i} \right)^2\right)= \prod_{=1}^n \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{u_{t,}}{\sigma_i} \right)^2\right). \] Introducing asymmetry, generalizes straightforwardly \[ \prod_{=1}^n \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{u_{t,}}{\sigma_i(u_{t,})} \right)^2\right). \] \(\sigma_i(u_{t,}) = \sigma_{L,}\) \(u_{t,} \leq 0\) \(\sigma_i(u_{t,}) = \sigma_{R,}\) \(u_{t,} > 0\). what: Demographic Approach to Species Distribution Model
title: Demographic Approach to Species Distribution Model
text: xsdm R package integrates concepts stochastic demography species distribution modelling (SDM). Instead treating environmental conditions static snapshot, xsdm uses multi-year environmental time-series together species presence/absence records : Reconstruct species’ fundamental ecological niche via maximum-likelihood estimation. Account inter-annual climate variability estimating niche breadth position. Project species’ potential geographic range current future climate scenarios. statistical underpinning described : Berti, E., Robles Fernández, .L., Rosenbaum, B., Peterson, T.., Soberón, J., & Reuman, D.C. (2025). impacts climate variability niche concept distributions species. bioRxiv. https://doi.org/10.1101/2024.10.30.621023

title: Demographic Approach to Species Distribution Model
text: Install latest version directly GitHub: xsdm requires R ≥ 4.1 links C++ via Rcpp/RcppParallel, working compiler (e.g. Rtools Windows, Xcode CLT macOS) needed.
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("xsdm-project/xsdm-devel")

what: 1 · Load and prepare environmental time series
title: Demographic Approach to Species Distribution Model
text: xsdm expects bioclimatic time-series rasters — one SpatRaster per variable layer representing one year (time step). built-data cover southern New Mexico, USA, 39 years (1980–2018) using CHELSA v2.1 bio1 (mean annual temperature) bio12 (annual precipitation). Take look temporal means standard deviations environmental variables:
code: library(xsdm) library(terra)  bio_1 <- terra::unwrap(example_1$bio01) bio_1 <- bio_1 / 100  bio_12 <- terra::unwrap(example_1$bio12) bio_12 <- bio_12 / 100 m_bio_1 <- terra::app(bio_1, mean) m_bio_12 <- terra::app(bio_12, mean) sd_bio_1 <- terra::app(bio_1, sd) sd_bio_12 <- terra::app(bio_12, sd) par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(3, 3, 2, 4)) terra::plot(m_bio_1, main = "Mean, bio1", xlab = "x", ylab = "y") terra::plot(m_bio_12, main = "Mean, bio12", xlab = "x", ylab = "y") terra::plot(sd_bio_1, main = "SD, bio1", xlab = "x", ylab = "y") terra::plot(sd_bio_12, main = "SD, bio12", xlab = "x", ylab = "y")

title: Demographic Approach to Species Distribution Model
text: Load occurrence data visualize detections non-detections top mean temperature map:
code: d <- example_1$occ_df pts_0 <- terra::vect(d[d$presence == 0, ], geom = c("lon", "lat"),                      crs = terra::crs(m_bio_1)) pts_1 <- terra::vect(d[d$presence == 1, ], geom = c("lon", "lat"),                      crs = terra::crs(m_bio_1)) par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(3, 3, 2, 4)) terra::plot(m_bio_1, main = "Non-detections", xlab = "x", ylab = "y") terra::plot(pts_0, add = TRUE, col = "black", pch = 20, cex = 0.2) terra::plot(m_bio_1, main = "Detections", xlab = "x", ylab = "y") terra::plot(pts_1, add = TRUE, col = "red", pch = 20, cex = 0.2)

title: Demographic Approach to Species Distribution Model
text: env_data_array() extracts stacks environmental values locations given presence/absence data frame, returning 3-D array (locations × time × variables).
code: env_data <- list(bio_1 = bio_1, bio_12 = bio_12) env_dat <- env_data_array(env_data, occ = d) dim(env_dat) #> [1] 4000   39    2 occ <- d$presence

title: Demographic Approach to Species Distribution Model
text: optimize_likelihood() runs multiple optimizations Latin-hypercube starting points returns every solution sorted decreasing log-likelihood:
code: result <- optimize_likelihood(   env_dat    = env_dat,   occ        = occ,   num_starts = 20L,   parallel   = FALSE,   verbose    = FALSE )  head(result$solutions[, c("start_id", "loglik", "convergence")]) #>   start_id    loglik convergence #> 1       11 -1042.503           4 #> 2        5 -1042.503           4 #> 3       18 -1042.503           4 #> 4       16 -1042.503           4 #> 5       17 -1042.503           4 #> 6        2 -1042.503           4 result$best$loglik #> [1] -1042.503

title: Demographic Approach to Species Distribution Model
text: Convert best math-scale parameter vector biologically interpretable scale plot inferred log growth–environment function:
code: best_bio <- math_to_bio(result$best$par)  par(mfrow = c(1, 2)) interpret_parameters(   best_bio,   plot_indices = c(1, 2),   env_dat      = env_dat,   occ          = occ )

title: Demographic Approach to Species Distribution Model
text: Use fitted parameters project habitat suitability map full raster extent:
code: hab_suit <- habitat_suitability(   param_list = best_bio,   env_list   = env_data ) terra::plot(hab_suit, main = "Habitat suitability", xlab = "x", ylab = "y")

title: Converts parameters from the biological scale to the math (unconstrained) scale — bio_to_math

text: named numeric vector math scale, ordered per   `make_mask_names(p)`.

title: Converts parameters from the biological scale to the math (unconstrained) scale — bio_to_math
text: Transformations: - `mu`      : identity - `sigltil` : `log()` - `sigrtil` : `log()` - `ctil`    : identity - `pd`      : `logit()` - `o_mat`   : lower-triangular parameters recovered via               `extract_orthogonal_matrix_parameters()` (see Details) `o_mat` entries mapped vector via principal matrix logarithm, .e., one (skew-symmetric) matrices S `o_mat = expm(S)`. math-scale parameters `o_par` strictly lower-triangular elements `S`. `p = 1`, `o_par` entries. Note, however, principal logarithm defined special orthogonal matrices (even though matrices image matrix exponential), function may fail valid `o_mat` inputs.

title: Converts parameters from the biological scale to the math (unconstrained) scale — bio_to_math
code: ## --- p = 1 (no o_par entries) --- mu1 <- 10 sigltil1 <- 1.2 sigrtil1 <- 0.8 bio_parameters <- list(   mu      = c(mu1),   sigltil = c(sigltil1),   sigrtil = c(sigrtil1),   ctil    = 0.3,   pd      = 0.85,   o_mat   = matrix(1, 1, 1) # 1x1 orthogonal ) math1 <- bio_to_math(bio_parameters) # Canonical names names(math1) #> [1] "mu1"      "sigltil1" "sigrtil1" "ctil"     "pd"       # Back to biological scale math_parameters <- math_to_bio(math1) all.equal(math_parameters$mu, bio_parameters$mu) #> [1] TRUE all.equal(math_parameters$sigltil, bio_parameters$sigltil) #> [1] TRUE all.equal(math_parameters$sigrtil, bio_parameters$sigrtil) #> [1] TRUE all.equal(math_parameters$ctil, bio_parameters$ctil) #> [1] TRUE all.equal(math_parameters$pd, bio_parameters$pd) #> [1] TRUE  ## --- p = 2 (includes one o_par) --- mu2 <- c(11, 5) sigltil2 <- c(1.1, 1.5) sigrtil2 <- c(1.4, 1.3) ctil2 <- -0.2 pd2 <- 0.9 o_par2 <- 0.25 O2 <- build_orthogonal_matrix(o_par2) bio_parameters_2d <- list(   mu      = mu2,   sigltil = sigltil2,   sigrtil = sigrtil2,   ctil    = ctil2,   pd      = pd2,   o_mat   = O2 ) math_parameters_2d <- bio_to_math(bio_parameters_2d) # check canonical name order produced by make_mask_names(2) identical(names(math_parameters_2d), names(make_mask_names(2))) #> [1] TRUE

what: Build an orthogonal matrix from a real-parameter vector — build_orthogonal_matrix
title: Build an orthogonal matrix from a real-parameter vector — build_orthogonal_matrix
text: Constructs \(k \times k\) orthogonal matrix \(O\) exponentiating skew-symmetric matrix \(S\) built assigning lower-triangular entries input vector. Specifically, function sets \(S_{ij}\) (\(>j\)) `entries`, mirrors enforce \(S = L - L^\top\), returns `expm::expm(S)`, guaranteed orthogonal \(\exp(S)\) orthogonal whenever \(S\) real skew-symmetric.

title: Build an orthogonal matrix from a real-parameter vector — build_orthogonal_matrix
code: build_orthogonal_matrix(entries)

title: Build an orthogonal matrix from a real-parameter vector — build_orthogonal_matrix
text: entries numeric vector (possibly `NULL`). `NULL`, returns `1 x 1` identity. Otherwise, length must \(n = k(k-1)/2\) integer \(k \ge 2\), supplying strictly lower-triangular entries skew-symmetric generator.

title: Build an orthogonal matrix from a real-parameter vector — build_orthogonal_matrix
text: `k x k` orthogonal matrix. `entries` `NULL`, returns   `matrix(1, 1, 1)` (identity).

title: Build an orthogonal matrix from a real-parameter vector — build_orthogonal_matrix
text: dimension `k` inferred `length(entries)` via relation \(n = k(k-1)/2\), `length(entries)` must equal triangular number. - Dimension inference uses \(k = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1 + 8n}}{2}\)   \(n = \text{length(entries)}\). \(k\) integer, input   invalid error thrown. - Orthogonality follows fact \(S^\top = -S\) implies   \(\exp(S)^\top \exp(S) = \). - Note function actually returns special orthogonal matrix, .e.,   determinant +1.

title: Build an orthogonal matrix from a real-parameter vector — build_orthogonal_matrix
code: # 1x1 identity (NULL input) build_orthogonal_matrix(NULL) #>      [,1] #> [1,]    1  # 2x2 orthogonal matrix from one parameter O2 <- build_orthogonal_matrix(0.0) all.equal(t(O2) %*% O2, diag(2)) # should be TRUE #> [1] TRUE  # 3x3 example: length(entries) = 3 (= 3*2/2), so k = 3 O3 <- build_orthogonal_matrix(c(0.1, -0.2, 0.3)) all.equal(t(O3) %*% O3, diag(3), tolerance = 1e-10) #> [1] TRUE

title: Tool to help interpret xsdm model parameters — interpret_parameters
text: env_dat occ provided, two panels drawn side side: left growth-environment function shown together environmental values presence locations (occ == 1); right function shown together environmental values non-detections (occ == 0). panels share identical contour breaks (bivariate case) identical axes (univariate case), two directly comparable. log growth-environment function determined inference affine transformation \(g = f(e) + b\) \(> 0\). contours therefore unlabelled output; shape interpretively meaningful. code function $$y(e) = -\sum_i \left( \frac{[u_i]_+}{\sigma^R_i}                            + \frac{[u_i]_-}{\sigma^L_i} \right)^2 ,       \quad u = O^{T} (e - \mu),$$ always \(\le 0\), attains maximum 0 \(e = \mu\), decreases without bound \(e\) moves away \(\mu\). Consequently numeric values y-axis univariate plot numeric values image colors bivariate plot carry units .

text: Creates virtual species probability--detection map based environmental time-series data set species-specific parameters, samples presence/absence points based user-defined probability threshold.

title: Generate a virtual species probability map with presence/absence sampling — vsp
code: vsp(param_list, env_data, size_presence, size_absence, threshold = 0.5)

title: Generate a virtual species probability map with presence/absence sampling — vsp
text: param_list named list biological‑scale parameters required `log_prob_detect()`. Must include `mu`, `sigltil`, `sigrtil`, `ctil`, `pd`, `o_mat`. Values like `sigltil`/`sigrtil` can `Inf`. env_data named list time‑series raster objects (e.g., `terra` package). element must `SpatRaster` geometry number layers. size_presence Integer. Number sample points draw cells detection probability **exceeds** `threshold`. size_absence Integer. Number sample points draw cells detection probability **less equal ** `threshold`. threshold Numeric `[0, 1]`. Probability cutoff used distinguish presence vs. absence sampling areas. Default `0.5`.

title: Generate a virtual species probability map with presence/absence sampling — vsp
text: tibble columns `lon`, `lat`, `presence` (0/1), row   corresponds sampled point. presence/absence drawn   binomial distribution using habitat suitability value success   probability.

title: Generate a virtual species probability map with presence/absence sampling — vsp
text: Internally function: Computes habitat suitability raster using `habitat_suitability()`. Splits raster two layers based `threshold`:         cells prob > threshold (presence pool) ≤ threshold (absence pool). Samples `size_presence` `size_absence` points pool         (without replacement), probabilities proportional suitability value. Generates binomial outcome sampled point using suitability         probability success.

title: xsdm: Demographic Approach to Species Distribution Model — xsdm-package

title: xsdm 1.0.0
text: End--end C++ likelihood chain. optimization hot path (loglik_math, loglik_bio, log_prob_detect, math_to_bio, create_param_vector_masked, like_neg_ltsgr) now driven entirely C++ kernels via thin R wrappers. pure-R reference implementations preserved non-exported *_r functions R/internals.R used test suite assert numerical parity tol = 1e-6. optimize_likelihood() profile_likelihood() drive ucminfcpp::ucminfcpp_xptr() pure-C++ XPtr factory (make_loglik_math_xptr_cpp), R callbacks inner loop. Parameter masking fully integrated: subset canonical parameter vector can fixed optimization, remaining free parameters optimized end--end math scale. Profile-likelihood name propagation fix. profile_likelihood() now preserves free-parameter names across ucminf boundary, profile rows reliably labelled parameter held fixed.

title: xsdm 1.0.0
text: New canonical exported names (thin C++ wrappers): like_neg_ltsgr_cpp longer exported; remains unexported back-compat alias (xsdm:::like_neg_ltsgr_cpp) forwards like_neg_ltsgr. New code call like_neg_ltsgr directly. dist_between_params() now backed pure-C++ implementation src/dist_between_params.cpp builds pairing cost matrix solves linear sum assignment problem via clean-room Hungarian / Kuhn–Munkres routine (O(n^3) potentials variant Kuhn 1955, Munkres 1957, Jonker & Volgenant 1987). code taken clue package, avoiding GPL-2 / AGPL-3 licence mismatch. pure-R reference xsdm:::dist_between_params_r continues call clue::solve_LSAP() used parity tests tests/testthat/test-dist_between_params_r_vs_cpp.R. legacy brute-force reference distance_between_params() also preserved (non-exported) xsdm:::distance_between_params_r.

title: xsdm 1.0.0
text: optimize_likelihood(), start_parms(), get_start_parms_() now reject num_starts < 3 early informative checkmate error, rather crashing inside sobol::sobol_design() (segfaults nseq = 0 returns malformed vector nseq = 1). start_parms() runtime: sobol::sobol_design output defensively coerced data.frame, protecting future behaviour changes.

title: xsdm 1.0.0
text: terra tibble moved Suggests: Imports:. used unconditionally mainline package code (env_data_array(), habitat_suitability(), start_parms(), vsp()). src/*.hpp headers renamed src/*.h satisfy R CMD check’s “Subdirectory ‘src’ contains” warning. New R-CMD-check.yaml GitHub workflow runs R CMD check ---cran Ubuntu (release / devel / oldrel-1 / oldrel-2), Windows, macOS. Test suite: 1006 tests, full suite ~9s, green; parity tests live tests/testthat/test-*_r_vs_cpp.R.
